text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Genitourinary Medical Oncology

**CHIEF COMPLAINT**
Established patient with metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma presenting for routine restaging assessment on cabozantinib. Also follow-up of recently evaluated low back pain.

---

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**

Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 64-year-old Nigerian-American woman, retired nurse, with a history of stage IV (T3b N1 M1) clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) of the right kidney with 20% sarcomatoid differentiation, Fuhrman grade 4, now approximately 18 months from initial diagnosis and 9 months into second-line therapy with cabozantinib. She presents today for a scheduled comprehensive restaging assessment.

To briefly summarize her clinical course: At initial diagnosis, she presented with a 14.2 cm right renal mass with macroscopic tumor thrombus in the right renal vein (Mayo grade 2), a single right hilar retroperitoneal lymph node metastasis (2.4 cm), and bilateral pulmonary nodules (largest 1.8 cm, right lower lobe). She underwent open cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection approximately 18 months ago, achieving an R0 resection; pathology confirmed pT3b pN1 M1, with 1 of 12 nodes positive (the right hilar node with extranodal extension). She recovered from surgery largely uneventfully, with a postoperative ileus that resolved by day 8 and mild anemia managed with oral iron.

She was initiated on first-line ipilimumab 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab 3 mg/kg every 3 weeks (CheckMate 214 regimen), chosen in part because of the sarcomatoid histology, a subgroup that demonstrated particularly robust benefit in the CheckMate 214 data, and a CPS of 12 on PD-L1 testing. She completed 4 cycles of the combination. After cycle 2, she developed immune-related colitis (Grade 2) managed with oral prednisone; ipilimumab was permanently discontinued and nivolumab was restarted after steroid taper. Restaging imaging at 3 months showed a mixed response — the dominant right lower lobe (RLL) nodule appeared slightly larger (initially attributed to possible pseudoprogression), while the other target lesions decreased. Confirmatory imaging at 6 months, however, showed unambiguous progression of that dominant lesion, two new right middle lobe pulmonary nodules, and a new right paracaval lymph node, consistent with true disease progression. Nivolumab was discontinued.

Approximately 9 months into her overall course (around 6 months after diagnosis), she was transitioned to second-line cabozantinib, chosen specifically because of the MET amplification (copy number 8.2) identified on her Foundation One CDx-equivalent tumor NGS panel — cabozantinib's dual VEGFR2/MET inhibition was felt to offer particular activity given this molecular feature and her sarcomatoid histology. She was started at 60 mg orally once daily. Within the first 2 months, she developed Grade 2 hand-foot skin reaction (HFSR) with painful erythema and blistering over the lateral right foot, and the dose was reduced to 40 mg daily; HFSR improved to Grade 1. She also experienced Grade 2 diarrhea (TKI-related, not immune-mediated) managed with loperamide and dietary modification, and Grade 2 hypertension managed by uptitrating her amlodipine from 5 to 10 mg daily.

Since dose reduction to cabozantinib 40 mg, she has done very well. The 3-month restaging scan showed stable disease with a minor response in all measured lesions — the dominant RLL nodule decreased from 2.6 cm to 2.3 cm and the right paracaval node responded notably from 1.1 cm to 0.7 cm. The 6-month restaging scan confirmed continued slow but steady shrinkage across all disease sites, with the right paracaval lymph node nearly completely resolved. She has now been on cabozantinib for approximately 9 months.

At her last interval visit (4-6 weeks ago), a new complaint of low back pain (Grade 1-2) over the prior 4 weeks prompted an MRI lumbar spine, which demonstrated multilevel degenerative disc disease at L4-L5 and no evidence of vertebral or epidural metastatic disease. The back pain has responded well to physical therapy and cautious use of naproxen. Today's visit includes review of the most recent restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis.

Today, the patient reports feeling overall well. She describes a modest but meaningful improvement in energy and functional capacity compared to earlier in her disease course. She has returned to light gardening, which she considers a meaningful quality-of-life marker. The back pain has substantially improved — she estimates approximately 70% better since beginning physical therapy, and she takes naproxen only occasionally now. She reports no new symptoms to suggest new sites of metastatic disease: no hemoptysis, no neurological symptoms, no bone pain beyond the chronic low back discomfort, no visual changes, no headaches, no abdominal pain, no early satiety.

---

**REVIEW OF SYSTEMS**

Constitutional: No significant fatigue (minimal, Grade 0-1). No fever, chills, or night sweats. Weight stable (patient reports no unintentional weight loss).
Dermatologic: Ongoing mild hyperkeratosis of bilateral palms and soles (Grade 1 HFSR), non-painful; manageable with emollient therapy. No new blistering. No rash elsewhere.
Gastrointestinal: Occasional loose stools (1-2 per day above baseline), Grade 1, managed well with as-needed loperamide; no frank diarrhea. No nausea or vomiting. No abdominal cramping. No rectal bleeding. Appetite is normal.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain. No exertional dyspnea. Blood pressure controlled at home; patient monitoring twice daily.
Pulmonary: No cough, no hemoptysis, no worsening dyspnea on exertion. No pleuritic chest pain.
Musculoskeletal: Improving low back pain as noted above; no new joint pain or myalgias.
Neurological: No headaches, no focal neurological deficits, no memory changes.
Endocrine: No symptoms of thyroid dysfunction. On levothyroxine with previously documented immune-related hypothyroidism (subclinical).
Genitourinary: No dysuria. No hematuria (expected given prior nephrectomy). Stable urine output.
Psychiatric: Patient coping well. No mood disturbances reported.

---

**PAST MEDICAL HISTORY**
(Established; reviewed and confirmed with patient)
1. Stage IV clear cell RCC, right kidney, with 20% sarcomatoid differentiation — as described above
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — well-controlled, on metformin; A1c not checked today, last was within acceptable range
3. Hypertension — managed with amlodipine 10 mg daily (uptitrated during cabozantinib therapy)
4. Immune-related hypothyroidism (post-nivolumab) — on levothyroxine replacement
5. Lumbar spondylosis (L4-L5, degenerative disc disease) — recently confirmed by MRI, no metastatic involvement
6. Prior postoperative ileus post-nephrectomy — resolved, no sequelae
7. Solitary left kidney (post-right nephrectomy) — with consequent CKD stage 3a (eGFR consistently in the mid-40s to upper 40s range)

**SOCIAL HISTORY**
Retired registered nurse; former hospital-based practice. Non-smoker (lifetime). No alcohol use. No illicit drug use. Lives with her husband; adult children nearby. Good social support. Active in community and church activities. Has returned to light gardening and daily walking as tolerated.

**FAMILY HISTORY**
Mother: deceased, cardiovascular disease. Father: deceased, prostate cancer in his 70s. One sibling with type 2 diabetes. No known family history of renal cell carcinoma, VHL disease, hereditary paraganglioma-pheochromocytoma syndrome, or other hereditary cancer syndromes. Germline testing was performed at diagnosis and was negative for VHL, FLCN, MET, SDHB, SDHC, SDHD, and PTEN pathogenic variants.

**ALLERGIES**
No known drug allergies. No latex allergy documented.

**MEDICATIONS**
1. Cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 40 mg orally once daily (dose-reduced from 60 mg due to Grade 2 HFSR)
2. Levothyroxine (Synthroid) 75 mcg orally once daily (dose adjusted from 50 mcg at approximately 4-month mark; for immune-related hypothyroidism)
3. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily (type 2 diabetes, held on days of IV contrast imaging per institutional protocol)
4. Amlodipine 10 mg orally once daily (hypertension; uptitrated from 5 mg during cabozantinib therapy)
5. Loperamide 2 mg orally as needed (up to 8 mg/day) for loose stools/diarrhea
6. Urea 40% cream applied topically to bilateral palms and plantar surfaces twice daily (HFSR maintenance)
7. Naproxen 375 mg orally as needed (low back pain; used cautiously with vigilance for renal function, not daily)
8. Aspirin 81 mg orally once daily (cardiovascular prophylaxis; patient preference)

---

**PHYSICAL EXAMINATION**

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 134/80 mmHg (left arm, seated), heart rate 72 bpm (regular), temperature 36.8°C, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, SpO2 98% on room air, weight 81.2 kg (stable), height 165 cm, BMI 29.8.

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Conversational, appropriate affect. English with slight Nigerian-British accent. Dressed and groomed appropriately.

Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. Conjunctivae mildly pale (consistent with chronic mild anemia). No oral mucositis. Dentition intact. Thyroid non-enlarged, non-tender, no palpable nodule.

Lymph Nodes: No palpable cervical, supraclavicular, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. S1 and S2 normal. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. No lower extremity edema.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rhonchi, or crackles. Symmetric chest expansion. No dullness to percussion.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Well-healed right flank surgical scar (nephrectomy). No palpable hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable abdominal or flank mass. No rebound tenderness. Normoactive bowel sounds.

Musculoskeletal: No midline spinal tenderness on percussion. Paraspinal muscle tenderness at the L4-L5 level (mild, bilateral, consistent with degenerative disease). Full range of motion of upper and lower extremities. Strength 5/5 throughout all four extremities. No joint swelling or erythema.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Gait steady and coordinated. Romberg negative. Sensation intact to light touch in bilateral lower extremities. Reflexes 2+ and symmetric.

Dermatologic: Bilateral palms: mild diffuse hyperkeratosis, no active erythema or blistering, non-tender to palpation. Bilateral plantar surfaces: mild hyperkeratosis with mild callusing, no ulceration. Grade 1 HFSR consistent with prior assessments. No new skin changes elsewhere. No rash.

Psychological/Mood: Patient appears in good spirits. Positive outlook regarding her disease course. Expresses gratitude for symptom control and ability to engage in daily activities.

---

**LABORATORY RESULTS**
(Obtained today, reviewed at time of visit)

Complete Blood Count:
- Hemoglobin: 10.6 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia; stable from prior assessments — 10.2 g/dL three months ago, 10.4 g/dL six weeks ago; chronic, multifactorial: TKI effect, solitary kidney, prior surgery)
- MCV: 83 fL (normocytic)
- White blood cell count: 5.6 x10^9/L (normal differential)
- Absolute neutrophil count: 2.8 x10^9/L (adequate)
- Platelets: 198 x10^9/L (normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L, Chloride: 102 mEq/L, Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
- BUN: 22 mg/dL, Creatinine: 1.45 mg/dL (eGFR approximately 43 mL/min/1.73m2 by CKD-EPI; stable, consistent with expected solitary kidney function)
- Glucose: 108 mg/dL (acceptable; patient on metformin)
- Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL, Direct bilirubin: 0.2 mg/dL
- AST: 28 U/L, ALT: 31 U/L (normal; no cabozantinib hepatotoxicity)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 72 U/L (normal)
- Albumin: 3.9 g/dL
- LDH: 188 U/L (within normal limits; previously mildly elevated at diagnosis)
- Corrected calcium: 9.2 mg/dL (normal)

Thyroid Function:
- TSH: 3.5 mIU/L (normal, euthyroid on current levothyroxine 75 mcg; target range achieved)
- Free T4: within normal limits (not explicitly repeated given normal TSH)

Urine:
- Spot urine protein/creatinine ratio: 0.18 (no clinically significant proteinuria; below the 0.5 threshold of concern with VEGFR inhibitor therapy)

Lipase: Not checked today (no clinical concern).

---

**IMAGING RESULTS**

Restaging CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with IV Contrast (performed within the past 2 weeks, reviewed in detail today):

Comparing to the prior restaging scan approximately 3 months ago:

Pulmonary disease: The dominant right lower lobe pulmonary metastasis has continued its gradual downward trajectory, now measuring 2.0 cm compared to 2.3 cm three months ago and 2.6 cm at cabozantinib initiation. This lesion has been slowly but consistently shrinking over the 9-month course of cabozantinib. The second right lower lobe nodule remains at 0.4 cm (unchanged). The left lower lobe target nodule has decreased slightly to 0.3 cm. The two right middle lobe nodules previously identified as new sites of disease at progression on nivolumab have continued to diminish — now 0.3 cm and 0.2 cm, tracking towards resolution. No new pulmonary nodules. No pleural effusion. No cavitation.

Retroperitoneal/nodal disease: The right paracaval lymph node, which had been as large as 1.1 cm at the time of cabozantinib initiation and decreased to 0.7 cm at 3-month assessment and 0.6 cm at 6-month assessment, is now no longer measurable — it has essentially undergone a near-complete response. No new retroperitoneal or pelvic lymphadenopathy.

Abdomen/pelvis: Status post right radical nephrectomy. The right renal fossa is well-defined without evidence of local recurrence. No new hepatic lesions. No adrenal masses. No ascites. The contralateral (left) kidney appears structurally normal without hydronephrosis, and the left renal parenchyma appears intact.

Skeleton: No new lytic or sclerotic bone lesions identified within the field of view. Degenerative changes at L4-L5 are again noted, consistent with MRI findings (no metastatic changes).

Overall sum of measurable target lesion diameters today: approximately 2.7 cm across the three target lesions, down from 3.7 cm at cabozantinib initiation and compared to 3.2 cm three months ago — representing a cumulative 27% reduction from the cabozantinib baseline. This continues to fall just short of the conventional 30% threshold for a formal partial response, but the direction and magnitude of response are clearly meaningful and unambiguous.

MRI Lumbar Spine (performed approximately 4-6 weeks ago in response to new back pain; reviewed today for completeness):
Multilevel lumbar degenerative disc disease, most pronounced at L4-L5. No evidence of vertebral metastatic disease, pathological fracture, or epidural involvement. No cord compression. This was highly reassuring and low back pain management has been directed accordingly.

---

**ASSESSMENT AND PLAN**

1. **Metastatic clear cell RCC (stage IV, T3b N1 M1, 20% sarcomatoid differentiation, Fuhrman grade 4) — second-line cabozantinib, durable stable disease, 9 months on therapy:**

 Ms. Okonkwo continues to demonstrate durable stable disease — approaching a near-partial response — on cabozantinib 40 mg daily, now 9 months into this regimen and approximately 18 months from her initial diagnosis. This is a gratifying and clinically meaningful outcome, particularly given the aggressive pathological features of her tumor (high-grade sarcomatoid differentiation, Fuhrman grade 4, renal vein invasion with extranodal extension from her nodal metastasis). The consistent and progressive reduction across all measurable disease sites — including near-complete resolution of the right paracaval lymph node that was a new site of progression at the time of nivolumab failure — gives me substantial confidence that this represents genuine, durable disease control rather than any transient stabilization.

 The MET amplification identified on her Foundation One CDx panel (copy number 8.2) at diagnosis remains scientifically interesting. Cabozantinib's dual MET and VEGFR2 inhibition likely contributes to the depth and durability of her response, as MET-driven pathways have been implicated in sarcomatoid transformation and aggressive RCC biology. While prospective data specifically in MET-amplified RCC are limited, the preclinical rationale and her clinical course are consistent with the expected enhanced sensitivity.

 Plan:
 - Continue cabozantinib 40 mg orally once daily. No dose modification indicated today. The patient is tolerating the current dose well and has expressed a preference to remain at 40 mg rather than attempt re-escalation to 60 mg, citing satisfactory disease control and acceptable quality of life. I discussed with her that the 40 mg dose is providing meaningful and ongoing benefit, and that dose re-escalation is not necessary for efficacy in her case. We will revisit this discussion only if there is evidence of disease progression.
 - Continue all supportive medications as listed above.
 - Schedule next restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in 3 months.
 - I advised her to continue monitoring her blood pressure twice daily at home and contact the clinic if readings exceed 150/95 mmHg on two consecutive measurements.
 - No bone-modifying agents (denosumab or zoledronic acid) are indicated at this time — there is no evidence of osseous metastatic disease on today's imaging.
 - Reviewed the importance of sun protection and appropriate footwear to minimize HFSR exacerbation.

2. **Cabozantinib-related toxicities — Grade 1 HFSR, Grade 1 intermittent diarrhea, controlled hypertension:**

 All toxicities are at Grade 1 or below and well-controlled. HFSR has stabilized at a non-functional, asymptomatic level since the dose reduction to 40 mg. No further dose adjustment is warranted for HFSR management at this time. Diarrhea is intermittent and adequately managed with PRN loperamide and a low-residue diet. Hypertension is well-controlled on amlodipine 10 mg.

 Plan:
 - Continue urea 40% cream bilaterally to palms and soles twice daily.
 - Loperamide as needed for loose stools.
 - Continue amlodipine 10 mg daily; blood pressure follow-up as noted above.
 - I emphasized to the patient to contact the clinic promptly if HFSR worsens to blistering or if diarrhea increases to more than 4-6 loose stools per day above baseline, as dose modification may be required.

3. **Immune-related hypothyroidism (post-nivolumab) — stable, on levothyroxine:**

 TSH 3.5 mIU/L today — euthyroid on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. No dose adjustment needed. Continue current dose and recheck TSH in approximately 3 months at next clinic visit.

4. **Low back pain — degenerative lumbar disc disease, no osseous metastases:**

 Back pain is substantially improved with physical therapy (ongoing) and naproxen used as needed (not daily). MRI confirmed this as degenerative disease at L4-L5, without any evidence of vertebral metastasis or cord compromise. Patient is encouraged to continue physical therapy and core strengthening exercises. Naproxen should be used sparingly given her CKD stage 3a (solitary kidney), with hydration emphasized. She was reminded to hold naproxen on any day she may need IV contrast for imaging.

 Plan:
 - Continue physical therapy.
 - Use naproxen judiciously; counsel to minimize frequency.
 - No further spinal imaging needed at this time unless back pain worsens significantly, changes in character, or neurological symptoms develop.

5. **Type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension — stable comorbidities:**

 Diabetes remains well-controlled on metformin. Blood pressure controlled on amlodipine 10 mg. No changes to these regimens today. Reminded patient to hold metformin on the day of and day after any IV contrast imaging.

6. **Renal function — CKD stage 3a, solitary left kidney:**

 Creatinine stable at 1.45 mg/dL (eGFR ~43 mL/min/1.73m2). No proteinuria. No electrolyte disturbances. No evidence of TKI-related nephrotoxicity beyond baseline CKD. Continued vigilance regarding nephrotoxic medications (NSAIDs used sparingly; no contrast without appropriate hydration). IV hydration protocol to be followed for all future CT contrast studies.

7. **Anemia — Grade 1, chronic and stable:**

 Hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL, stable. Multifactorial etiology (cabozantinib, solitary kidney, prior surgery). No transfusion required. No ESA indicated in this oncologic context. Patient is not symptomatic from anemia. Continue to monitor.

8. **Disease monitoring and future planning:**

 The patient currently has no immediately eligible clinical trials at this institution for her disease state. We will continue to monitor for any newly opening trials, particularly in the MET-amplified RCC space or trials of novel agents in later-line settings. Third-line options upon eventual progression would include lenvatinib plus everolimus (based on Study 205 data), axitinib, or enrollment on a clinical trial. Belzutifan (HIF-2α inhibitor) may also be relevant in a later-line setting. I have made a note to discuss these options when they become clinically relevant.

 The patient is very engaged in her care, asks excellent questions, and demonstrates strong health literacy from her nursing background. She understood and verbalized all aspects of today's discussion, including the rationale for continuing cabozantinib at the current dose and the plan for ongoing monitoring.

Return to clinic: 3 months (or sooner if clinically indicated).
Estimated visit time with patient: 45 minutes.

Dictated by: Genitourinary Medical Oncology Attending Physician
Note reviewed by attending physician at time of visit.
